name: Kitchen Material Sorting Challenge
description: Gather 20 common kitchen items (salt, sugar, baking soda, vinegar, oil, water, flour, aluminum foil, plastic wrap, rubber band, wooden spoon, metal spoon, sponge, etc.) and sort them by multiple properties: state of matter (solid/liquid/gas), dissolves in water or not, conducts electricity or not, magnetic or not. Discover that materials that look similar (white powders) behave very differently, and that classification depends on which property you choose. Record results in a properties table.
materials: Cups of water for dissolving tests
Small battery and LED for conductivity tests (optional)
Data recording sheet with properties columns
learning_objectives: Classify materials by observable properties
Discover that similar-looking materials can have different properties
Record and organize property data in tables
Understand that classification systems depend on chosen criteria
variations: Younger learners: sort by only 2-3 properties (state, color, texture)
Advanced: predict properties before testing and check predictions
Extension: create a dichotomous key for identifying kitchen materials

name: Mystery Substance Properties Lab
description: Five labeled cups (A through E) contain different white powders: salt, sugar, baking soda, cornstarch, and flour. Using only observation and safe tests — appearance under magnifying glass, texture, dissolving in water, reaction with vinegar, reaction with iodine solution (from a pharmacy), and taste (salt and sugar only, with permission) — learners identify each substance by its unique combination of properties. Emphasizes that identification requires multiple tests, not just one.
materials: 5 white powders (salt, sugar, baking soda, cornstarch, flour)
learning_objectives: Use multiple property tests to identify unknown substances
Record observations systematically in a data table
Understand that a single test is insufficient for identification
Distinguish physical properties from chemical properties
variations: Add a sixth mystery powder (cream of tartar, baking powder) as a challenge
Blind version: learners design their own testing procedure before starting
Quantitative version: measure the mass that dissolves in a fixed volume of water

name: Build an Atom Model with Candy
description: Using different colored candies or beads to represent protons (red), neutrons (blue), and electrons (green), learners build physical models of atoms. Start with hydrogen (1 proton, 1 electron), then helium (2 protons, 2 neutrons, 2 electrons), and progress to carbon, oxygen, sodium, and chlorine. Arrange electrons in concentric rings representing energy levels (shells). Compare models and discover patterns: elements in the same column have the same number of outer electrons.
materials: Paper plates or cardboard circles for shells
learning_objectives: Identify the three subatomic particles and their locations
Build accurate atomic models for given elements
Recognize that the number of protons defines the element
Discover the pattern of electron arrangement in shells
variations: Younger learners: build only hydrogen, helium, and carbon
Advanced: build models showing isotopes of the same element
Extension: predict what element results from adding or removing protons

name: Periodic Table Scavenger Hunt
description: Given a set of clue cards describing element properties (e.g., 'I am a gas at room temperature and glow orange-red in a discharge tube. I am named after a Greek word meaning new.' Answer: Neon), learners race to find each element on the periodic table and record its symbol, atomic number, and group. Clues range from easy (describing common elements like iron and gold) to challenging (describing lesser-known elements by their properties and history). Builds familiarity with the table as a usable tool.
materials: Printed periodic table (one per learner or pair)
learning_objectives: Navigate the periodic table to locate elements efficiently
Connect element descriptions to periodic table positions
Recognize that element properties relate to their position in the table
Build familiarity with element names, symbols, and atomic numbers
variations: Team competition: first team to correctly identify all elements wins
Create-your-own: learners write clue cards for classmates
Digital version: use an interactive periodic table to find elements

name: Molecule Building with Toothpicks and Gumdrops
description: Using gumdrops (atoms of different colors for different elements) and toothpicks (chemical bonds), learners build three-dimensional models of molecules: water (H2O — bent shape), methane (CH4 — tetrahedral), carbon dioxide (CO2 — linear), ammonia (NH3 — pyramidal), and ethanol (C2H5OH). After building, compare shapes and discuss why molecules are not flat. Connect shape to properties: water is bent and polar, which is why it dissolves salt; CO2 is linear and nonpolar, which is why it is a gas.
materials: Gumdrops in multiple colors (assign one color per element)
Toothpicks (single for single bonds, doubled for double bonds)
learning_objectives: Build accurate 3D molecular models from chemical formulas
Recognize that molecules have specific three-dimensional shapes
Identify bond types (single, double) in model construction
Connect molecular shape to physical properties
variations: Simpler: build only water, methane, and CO2
Advanced: predict the shape before building, then check
Extension: compare a molecule built flat vs. 3D and discuss which is correct

name: Ionic vs. Covalent Compound Sort
description: Given a set of common substances (table salt, sugar, water, baking soda, iron nail, copper wire, diamond, rubbing alcohol, candle wax, glass), learners research the bonding type of each and sort into ionic, covalent, and metallic categories. Then test predictions by observing properties: does it dissolve in water? Does the solution conduct electricity? Is it brittle or malleable? Has it got a high or low melting point? Discover that bond type predicts physical properties.
materials: Cups of water for dissolving tests
learning_objectives: Classify substances by bonding type (ionic, covalent, metallic)
Predict physical properties from bonding type
Test predictions experimentally using property observations
Connect atomic-level bonding to macroscopic behavior
variations: Simpler: provide bonding type and focus only on property testing
Advanced: include borderline cases (network covalent like diamond)
Extension: research an unfamiliar substance and predict its bonding type from properties alone

name: Baking Soda and Vinegar Reaction Investigation
description: Not just 'pour and watch' — a systematic investigation of the baking soda and vinegar reaction. Learners vary the amount of baking soda (1 tsp, 2 tsp, 3 tsp) with a fixed amount of vinegar, capturing the gas produced in a balloon stretched over the bottle opening. Measure balloon circumference to quantify gas production. Weigh the system before and after (in a sealed container) to test conservation of mass. Identify the gas as carbon dioxide by testing whether it extinguishes a flame.
materials: String and ruler for balloon measurement
Small candle for gas identification test (adult supervised)
learning_objectives: Design a controlled experiment varying one factor at a time
Collect quantitative data about a chemical reaction
Test the law of conservation of mass experimentally
Identify a reaction product (CO2) using a chemical test
variations: Younger learners: focus on observation and description of the reaction
Advanced: calculate the theoretical gas volume and compare with measured
Extension: test other acids (lemon juice, soda) with baking soda and compare

name: Endothermic Ice Cream Making
description: Make ice cream in a bag using the endothermic process of salt dissolving in ice. Place cream, sugar, and vanilla in a small zip-lock bag inside a larger bag filled with ice and rock salt. Shake vigorously for 10-15 minutes. Measure the temperature of the ice-salt mixture at intervals — discover it drops well below 0 degrees C. The salt disrupts the ice crystal structure, and the endothermic dissolution absorbs heat from the cream mixture, freezing it. Delicious science.
materials: Gloves or towel (the bag gets very cold)
learning_objectives: Experience an endothermic process firsthand
Measure temperature changes during an endothermic reaction
Explain why salt makes ice colder (in terms of energy absorption)
Distinguish endothermic from exothermic processes
variations: Compare rock salt vs. table salt: which makes the mixture colder?
Vary the salt-to-ice ratio and measure temperature differences
Research other endothermic processes in everyday life

name: Red Cabbage pH Indicator Lab
description: Make a universal pH indicator by boiling chopped red cabbage in water and straining the purple liquid. Test household substances by adding drops of the indicator: lemon juice (red/pink = acidic), vinegar (red/pink), baking soda solution (blue/green = basic), soap solution (green = basic), bleach (yellow = very basic), milk (purple = near neutral), soda (pink = acidic). Create a color-pH reference chart. Discover that acids and bases are everywhere in the kitchen and bathroom.
materials: Household substances to test (lemon juice, vinegar, baking soda, soap, milk, soda, etc.)
learning_objectives: Create a pH indicator from natural materials
Classify household substances as acidic, basic, or neutral
Construct a color-pH reference chart from experimental data
Understand that acids and bases are common in everyday life
variations: Test rainwater, tap water, and distilled water to compare pH
Neutralization experiment: slowly add baking soda to vinegar and watch the color change through the pH spectrum
Compare cabbage indicator to commercial pH strips for accuracy

name: Polymer Lab — Make Your Own Slime
description: Create polymers by mixing white school glue (polyvinyl acetate) with borax solution (sodium tetraborate). The borax cross-links the polymer chains in the glue, transforming liquid glue into a stretchy, bouncy, semi-solid material. Vary the borax concentration (1 tsp, 2 tsp, 3 tsp per cup of water) and observe how the polymer properties change — from runny slime to firm putty. Connect to polymer chemistry: long molecular chains behave differently when they are free to slide vs. when they are cross-linked.
learning_objectives: Create a polymer through a cross-linking reaction
Observe how varying a reactant changes product properties
Understand the relationship between molecular structure and material behavior
Connect the concept of polymers to everyday materials (plastic, rubber, nylon)
variations: Compare slime made with glue vs. slime made with liquid starch (different cross-linker)
Measure quantitative properties: how far does it stretch before breaking? How high does a ball bounce?
Research: identify 5 polymers you use every day and find out what monomers they are made from
